summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Determined on the papers – where the wife seeks costs in relation to final property proceedings – where the husband made no submission as to costs – where the wife was self-represented throughout the proceedings – where the wife’s submissions did not address section 117 considerations – where the wife failed to adduce evidence in support of her submissions – where the wife did not produce a costs agreement in accordance with rule 12.13 – application for costs dismissed.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

INTRODUCTION

The husband, Mr Matthews, and the wife, Ms Norris have been engaged in family law litigation since 2015. Indeed, this litigation has endured for a period longer than their relationship.
Following a successful appeal to the Full Court, the contested property proceedings were allocated to my docket in August 2023. The trial commenced and was heard over five days in 2024; directions were made for the filing of written submissions and judgment was otherwise reserved.
The husband and the wife each filed Applications in a Proceeding to reopen the hearing, and those applications were listed for hearing.
Prior to that hearing in September 2025, the Court was informed that the husband had been declared bankrupt. A trustee in bankruptcy was appointed in September 2025. On 13 November 2025 the trustee in bankruptcy confirmed that he did not intend to participate in these proceedings on the basis that the estate was without funds and unable to meet the costs associated with the matter.
On 17 November 2025, the husband appeared in person and indicated to the Court that he did not wish to pursue his application for final property orders. That application for final property orders was subsequently dismissed.
The proceedings were adjourned to enable the wife to consider her position in circumstances where her Amended Response to Application for Final Orders filed 4 March 2024 remained on foot.
On 12 December 2025, I made orders dismissing the wife’s Amended Response to Final Orders filed 4 March 2024.
The wife now seeks orders for costs arising out of those property proceedings. She seeks an order that the husband pay “$450,000 in costs to be paid from the applicant’s superannuation fund to the respondent’s superannuation fund within 14 days of the orders”. In support of that application the wife relies upon her submissions filed 2 January 2026.
The husband has filed no submission in response to that of the wife.
These are my Reasons for Judgment with respect to that application.

BACKGROUND

The parties married in 2009 and separated in 2015. They were divorced in 2018. There is one child of the marriage, B, who is aged 15 years and lives with the husband. I otherwise refer to and rely upon the ex-tempore reasons for judgment dated 12 December 2025 [Matthews & Norris (No 3) [2025] FedCFamC1F 975] which details the history of the dispute.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

The costs provisions under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) were amended by Schedule 4, Part 1 of the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) (“the Amendment Act”) which came into effect as and from 10 June 2025. The effect of the amendment was to repeal s 117 of the Act and to implement Part XIVC of the Act, which at s 114UB specifically deals with costs orders.
The first question for determination is whether the provisions implemented pursuant to the Amendment Act apply to these proceedings. The relevant transitional provisions are set out in the Amendment Act at Schedule 4, Item 15 which provides as follows:-
The amendments of the Family Law Act 1975 made by Divisions 1 and 2 of this Part apply in relation to the following proceedings:
(a) proceedings instituted on or after the day this Division commences;

(b) proceedings instituted before, and not finally determined before, the day this Division commences, other than proceedings in respect of which a final hearing has commenced before that day.

Section 4(1) of the Act provides that “proceedings” are defined as follows:-
...
(b) otherwise—means a proceeding in a court, whether between parties or not, and includes cross‑proceedings or an incidental proceeding in the course of or in connection with a proceeding.
In circumstances where the final hearing commenced before the repeal of s 117 of the Act, that section continues to apply to the current application.
Section 117 of the Act provides that each party to proceedings is to bear their own costs unless there are circumstances which in the opinion of the Court justify the making of a costs order.
Section 117(2) of the Act provides that:-
(2) If, in proceedings under this Act, the court is of opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the court may, subject to subsections (2A), (4), (4A), (5) and (6) and the applicable Rules of Court, make such order as to costs and security for costs, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise, as the court considers just.
Section 117(2A) of the Act provides that in determining what, if any, order should be made under subsection (2), the Court must have regard to the following:-
(a) the financial circumstances of each of the parties to the proceedings;

(b) whether any party to the proceedings is in receipt of assistance by way of legal aid and, if so, the terms of the grant of that assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting the generality of the foregoing, the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters;

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether either party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer; and

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.

Should it be determined that it is just to make an order as to costs, costs are not awarded to punish the unsuccessful party, but rather, are compensatory in the sense that they are awarded to ameliorate the expense of the successful party resulting from them having been required to participate in the proceedings.
The discretion afforded by s 117 of the Act is broad and the relevant factors contained in s 117(2A) are not to be read in a restrictive way; any one of those factors may found an order for costs, but all factors must be considered and balanced (I and I [1995] FamCA 80; (1995) FLC 92-625).
The Court has a broad discretion as to the weight to be attributed to the factors set out in s 117(2A) (Medlon & Medlon (No. 6) (Indemnity Costs) [2015] FamCAFC 157; (2015) FLC 93-664 at [24]).
The method of calculation of costs is prescribed by r 12.17(1) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) ("the Rules"), which provides:-
(1) The court may order that a party is entitled to costs:
(a) of a specific amount; or
(b) as assessed on a particular basis (for example, party and party, solicitor and client or indemnity); or

(c) to be calculated in accordance with the method stated in the order; or

(d) for part of the proceeding, or part of an amount, assessed in accordance with Schedule 3.

Rule 12.17(3) sets out matters that may be considered in the calculation of costs, providing that:-
(3) In making an order under subrule (1), the court may consider the following:
(a) the importance, complexity or difficulty of the issues;

(b) the reasonableness of each party's behaviour in the proceeding including by having regard to the matters set out in subrule 12.08(2);

(c) the rates ordinarily payable to lawyers in comparable proceedings;

(d) whether a lawyer's conduct has been improper, unfair, unreasonable or disproportionate;

(e) the time properly spent on the proceeding, or in complying with pre-action procedures;

(f) whether expenses (paid or payable) are fair, reasonable and proportionate.

Having regard to the above matters, I consider that I hold a broad discretion in respect of matters relating to any costs orders.
The law with respect to indemnity costs is well settled; the Full Court observed in Kohan & Kohan [1992] FamCA 116; (1993) FLC 92-340 that the Court should not depart lightly from the ordinary rules with respect to costs. In the decision of Prantage & Prantage (2013) [2013] FamCAFC 105; 49 Fam LR 197, the Full Court confirmed that indemnity costs should only be awarded if the case has some special or unusual feature.
In Colgate-Palmolive v Cussons Pty Ltd [1993] FCA 536; (1993) 46 FCR 225, Sheppard J identified circumstances that might constitute special or unusual features so as to justify an award of indemnity costs. Usefully, Holden CJ in Munday v Bowman (1997) FLC 92-784 at 84,660 drew from the decision of Sheppard J the following examples:-
(a) Where it appears that an action has been commenced or continued in circumstances where a party properly advised should have known that he had no chance of success. In such cases the action must be presumed to have been commenced or continued for some ulterior motive or because of some wilful disregard of the known facts.

(b) Making allegations of fraud, knowing them to be false, and the making of irrelevant allegations of fraud.

(c) Evidence of particular misconduct causing loss of time to the court and to other parties.

(d) The making of allegations which ought never to have been made or the undue prolongation of a case by groundless contentions.

(e) An imprudent refusal of an offer to compromise.

(Citations omitted)
The category of circumstances which enliven the discretion to award indemnity costs are not closed; the circumstances do not need to come precisely within the examples provided by Sheppard J.

THE WIFE’S SUBMISSIONS

The submissions of the wife do not specifically address the considerations identified in s 117(2A) of the Act. Rather, those submissions list a series of criticisms or complaints as to husband’s conduct and the manner in which he has conducted the proceedings. For example, the wife submits that:

· The husband has dragged her through the Court for more than 10 years;

· The husband has committed acts of family violence since the commencement of the marriage and that such conduct continues to this day;

· She “is on pain medication for the rest of her life” due to injuries she contends were inflicted by the husband;

· The husband has misled the Court in the parenting proceedings as he has not permitted the child to spend time with the mother;

· The husband has been awarded all the assets from the marriage including superannuation;

· The husband wasted funds received from the sale of matrimonial assets and sold matrimonial property at a loss;

· The parties settled at a mediation and the husband “reneged” on the settlement;

· She has expended more than $500,000 in legal costs since the commencement of the proceedings;

· She does not accept that the husband’s bankruptcy is legitimate;

· The husband owns real property with his parents;

· The husband cannot be trusted;

· The husband has perjured himself in the proceedings;

· She, in contrast, has acted “honestly and with integrity” throughout the proceedings;

· The husband has more than $600,000 in superannuation and has withdrawn money from his self-managed superannuation fund for his personal use.

Many of the allegations made by the wife in her submissions are made without evidentiary foundation to support them or are not relevant to the issue before the Court. As a result, I consider that those submissions must be treated with caution.

SECTION 117(2A) CONSIDERATIONS

(a) The financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings

The wife made no submissions with respect to the parties’ financial circumstances other than her assertions as to the husband having retained and sold matrimonial assets, that he holds property interests with his parents, and that he has superannuation interests.
Th wife has made no submissions as to her own financial circumstances. Her last filed Financial Statement, which was filed on 4 March 2024, discloses that at that time the wife was a property manager and stay-at-home mother. She disclosed income of approximately $811 per week, superannuation interests of approximately $106,000, assets of $3,000 and liabilities of approximately $173,000.
The husband has been declared bankrupt. His trustee in bankruptcy declined the opportunity to participate in these proceedings having taken the view that the costs of the proceedings would outweigh any benefit in pursuing the litigation on behalf of the husband’s estate.

(b) Whether any party to the proceeding is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings

Neither the husband nor the wife is in receipt of legal aid.

(c) The conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings

In her written submissions, the wife was highly critical of the husband’s conduct. Those criticisms were largely directed to her allegations as to his conduct during the marriage. No findings have been made with respect to those matters.
The husband was represented by lawyers during the proceedings. He was generally compliant with orders and directions of the Court in relation to the conduct of the proceedings.
The wife contends that the husband did not make disclosure in relation to aspects of the dispute. Again, no findings were made with respect to those matters.
The wife was self-represented throughout the proceedings. She too was generally compliant with Court orders and directions. Significant time was expended in the course of the proceedings explaining court processes to her and she was afforded the indulgence of filing written closing submissions at the conclusion of the proceedings rather than providing oral submissions, which further protracted the proceedings.

(d) Whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the Court

This consideration is not relevant.

(e) Whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings

No submissions were made by the wife with respect to this consideration.
Ultimately, both parties’ applications for final property orders were dismissed. In effect, that leaves the parties in the position envisaged by the wife in her Amended Response to Application for Final Orders filed 4 March 2024, being that the parties shall each retain the property in their names and/or control. In circumstances where the husband sought orders for property adjustment as against the wife, I am satisfied that he has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings.

(f) Whether either party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to settle the proceedings

Again, no submission was made with respect to this consideration.
I note the wife’s submission that the parties resolved the matter in principle at mediation and that the husband reneged upon the alleged agreement.
There is no evidence before the Court as to any offers of settlement in writing or the terms of any offer or agreement made. Accordingly, I am in a position to make findings with respect to those matters.

DISCUSSION

Having regard to all of the above matters, I am not persuaded that there are circumstances to justify an order for costs.
In my view the wife’s application is misconceived, particularly given that she was self-represented throughout the proceedings before me. The wife has adduced no evidence to particularise her claim for costs in the sum of $450,000.
The wife’s claim appears to be for costs on an indemnity basis, although that is not stated in her written submissions. The wife has adduced no evidence as to legal costs incurred by her during the proceedings. Further, the wife has not produced a costs agreement between she and any lawyer engaged by her to assist in the proceedings, as is required by Rule 12.13(4) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021. In addition, the wife does not identify the source of power by which it is said that orders ought be made to withdraw funds from the husband’s superannuation entitlements to meet her costs claim.
Having regard to all of the above matters, I am not satisfied that there are circumstances that justify the Court making an order for costs. I am satisfied that each party should bear their own costs. Accordingly, I will dismiss the wife’s application for costs.
